phase1.explanation:
Character streams handle text files with proper encoding:

Reader (base class for reading characters):
• FileReader — reads characters from a file

Writer (base class for writing characters):
• FileWriter — writes characters to a file

Key features:
• Handle character encoding (Unicode)
• Read/write char or String
• More convenient than byte streams for text

Key methods:
• read() — reads single character (returns -1 at end)
• read(char[] buffer) — reads into char array
• write(String str) — writes a String
• write(char[] data) — writes char array

FileWriter modes:
• new FileWriter("file.txt") — overwrites
• new FileWriter("file.txt", true) — appends

Byte vs Character streams:
• Byte streams: raw bytes, any file
• Character streams: text files with encoding handling

When to use character streams:
• Reading/writing text files
• Any human-readable data
• Use BufferedReader/Writer for better performance

phase2.errorMessage: (No error — but the output is WRONG! File contains "AB", not "6566")
phase2.explanation:
FileWriter.write(int) writes the CHARACTER represented by the int, not the string representation!

fw.write(65) writes 'A' (ASCII 65), not the string "65".

To write numbers as text:
• fw.write("65")
• fw.write(Integer.toString(65))
• fw.write(String.valueOf(65))

Remember: FileWriter is for characters, not numbers!

FIX: Convert numbers to strings first:
fw.write(String.valueOf(65));  // Writes "65"

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates a text file called "notes.txt"
2. Writes three lines of text using FileWriter
3. Reads the file back using FileReader
4. Prints the content line by line

Use try-with-resources for both operations.
dialogueHints: Use FileWriter to write text, use FileReader to read it back
phase3.openingDialogue: Character streams — text handling made easy! 📝
